planting.note: Plant container-grown palms in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moist, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; pygmy date palm establishes slowly and resents root disturbance — handle the root ball carefully during planting. Apply palm-formulated slow-release fertilizer in spring and summer to prevent micronutrient deficiencies.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; pygmy date palm is one of the most elegant and widely used small palms in subtropical and tropical landscaping worldwide, prized for its graceful, arching, feathery pinnate fronds on a slender, slightly rough-textured trunk that gives it the appearance of a miniature tropical date palm — exactly the feeling of the tropics in a compact size. Typically reaching 6–12 feet in the landscape over many years, it fits where larger palms do not: in courtyards, as potted specimens on patios, as accent plants beside entrances and pools, and in shaded positions under taller trees where its tolerance for reduced light is valuable. Multi-stem specimens (3 trunks planted together) create a particularly lush, tropical effect. Spines on the lower fronds and petioles can cause injury — wear long sleeves when working near established palms. Apply specialized palm fertilizer formulated for Florida and Gulf Coast soils to prevent manganese and potassium deficiencies.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full shade to partial sun; lady palm is one of the most shade-tolerant of all palms and actually declines in full sun exposure in hot climates. It establishes slowly but requires virtually no maintenance once established.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full shade to partial sun — lady palm is one of the most shade-tolerant palms available and is one of the premier choices for deeply shaded landscape positions where other palms and most shrubs fail to thrive. The multiple slender stems with ringed, fibrous sheaths and glossy, fan-shaped, deeply divided dark-green leaves create an exceptionally refined, elegant clumping form that is unsurpassed for sophisticated tropical foliage effect in the shade garden. It grows very slowly — a well-established 10-year-old plant is a prized possession. Historically expensive due to slow production, named cultivars (including variegated forms) are highly collectible among palm enthusiasts and have been cultivated in Chinese and Japanese gardens for centuries. It is outstanding as a large container specimen indoors (one of the best indoor palms) or in shaded outdoor landscapes in zones 8–11. Water regularly but ensure good drainage — soggy roots cause decline.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs or young trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; strawberry guava is extremely adaptable to poor, acidic, and sandy soils. Note: it is considered invasive in Hawaii and subtropical areas with high rainfall — do not plant near natural areas in Hawaii, Florida, or other humid subtropical regions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; strawberry guava is a highly productive and flavorful small fruit tree or large shrub that produces abundant crops of 1-inch round fruits in red (the classic form) or yellow (Psidium cattleyanum var. lucidum, lemon guava) with an intensely sweet, slightly strawberry-like flavor — arguably the finest-flavored guava species, often preferred over the larger common guava by people who taste it. The glossy, dark-green evergreen leaves and smooth, mottled reddish-brown bark provide year-round ornamental value. In zone 9 it may suffer light frost damage but typically recovers well. It is extremely drought, heat, and poor-soil tolerant once established and produces fruit with minimal care. Important note: strawberry guava is listed as one of the world's 100 worst invasive species in subtropical humid regions — do not plant where it can escape to natural areas in Hawaii, Florida, and similar climates. In drier Gulf Coast locations it is less problematic.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, moist soil in full sun to partial shade; cherry of the Rio Grande is relatively slow growing and blooms most heavily in spring with fruit ripening in early summer. Self-fertile, but cross-pollination with a second plant increases fruit set.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, moist soil in full sun to partial shade; cherry of the Rio Grande is a Brazilian Eugenia species prized by subtropical fruit enthusiasts for its exceptional flavor — the dark red to purple, oblong, cherry-sized fruits have a rich, complex flavor that blends cherry, wine, and grape notes with a slight aromatic complexity that is unique in the subtropical fruit world. It is one of the most sought-after specialty fruits for zone 9 food gardens. The dense, glossy, evergreen foliage is attractive year-round and the showy white flowers appear in spring. It grows as a medium shrub or small tree and tolerates partial shade well, making it suitable as an understory specimen in the food forest. The fruit ripens from May through June on the Gulf Coast and must be harvested promptly as it ferments rapidly after picking. In zone 9a it may need frost protection in hard winters; established plants recover readily. One of the top five subtropical fruits for Gulf Coast gardens.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall or spring in any well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to full shade; dwarf yaupon is extraordinarily adaptable — it tolerates poor soil, wet soil, dry soil, heat, drought, salt spray, and air pollution, making it virtually indestructible in Gulf Coast landscapes. No fertilizer needed once established.

directions: Plant in any well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to full shade — dwarf yaupon holly is one of the most versatile, low-maintenance native shrubs for Gulf Coast landscapes, remaining permanently at 2–4 feet without pruning in contrast to the standard yaupon (Ilex vomitoria) which reaches 15–20 feet. The dense, mounding form with tiny, dark-green, evergreen leaves creates a refined, naturally compact habit that requires no shearing to maintain its shape. It is extraordinary in its tolerance of challenging conditions: extreme heat, extended drought, coastal salt spray, wet soils, deep shade, and urban pollution are all handled with equanimity. A large-scale mass planting of dwarf yaupon under mature live oaks creates a dramatic, maintenance-free understory planting that will look perfect for decades. Female plants ('Schillings Dwarf' is the most common selection) produce tiny red berries in winter that attract dozens of bird species. One of the few native shrubs that genuinely thrives in deep, dry shade.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fall or spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moist soil in partial shade to full sun; red bay grows naturally in moist, sandy, coastal soils and bottomlands. Note: red bay is devastated by laurel wilt disease caused by the redbay ambrosia beetle (Xyleborus glabratus) throughout much of its native range — check local extension for current recommendations.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately wet soil in partial shade to full sun; red bay is the native American aromatic bay of the coastal Southeast and Gulf Coast, producing glossy, lance-shaped, dark-green leaves with the same aromatic oils and culinary bay-laurel fragrance as the Mediterranean bay laurel (Laurus nobilis) — the leaves are used identically in Southern cooking for seasoning gumbos, soups, stews, and bean dishes. In fact, red bay has traditionally been used in Gulf Coast cuisine (particularly in Louisiana) for generations as the local bay leaf. It is a fast-growing native tree of coastal hammocks and stream margins that provides excellent wildlife value — the blue-black fruits are important food for migrating songbirds, and the palamedes swallowtail butterfly uses red bay as its exclusive larval host plant. Tragically, laurel wilt disease (spread by an invasive ambrosia beetle) has killed the vast majority of red bay trees throughout the Southeast — consult local extension services before planting.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in well-drained, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), humus-rich, moist soil slightly above grade in partial shade; camellia is critical about soil pH and drainage — it will not tolerate alkaline soil, heavy clay, or waterlogged conditions. Fall planting allows root establishment before the winter bloom season begins.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Japanese camellia is the quintessential flowering shrub of the American South, producing large, formal, rose-like flowers in red, pink, white, and bicolor combinations in late fall through early spring — when almost nothing else is in bloom. Over 30,000 named cultivars exist, ranging from single-flowered (most cold-hardy) to fully double (most spectacular). In zone 8–9 it is one of the most spectacular ornamental shrubs available: a mature, large-flowered camellia in full January bloom, covered in hundreds of perfect flowers, is one of the most breath-taking sights in any garden. It requires strongly acidic soil and will show iron and manganese deficiency chlorosis in neutral or alkaline conditions. Prune immediately after bloom in late spring before the following year's buds set. Scale insects and petal blight are the primary concerns — improve air circulation and treat scale with horticultural oil in late winter.

planting.note: Plant container-grown palms in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; fishtail palm handles more shade than most palms and performs well under the canopy of larger trees. The berries and fruit are extremely irritating to skin and mucous membranes — wear gloves when handling fallen fruit.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; clustering fishtail palm is one of the most distinctive and architecturally unusual of all tropical palms, producing large, bipinnate fronds composed of distinctive irregularly triangular leaflets that look exactly like the tails of fish — a completely different texture from the familiar pinnate or palmate fronds of most palms. The multiple, slender stems grow in tight clusters, producing a graceful, multi-trunked tropical form that is outstanding as a large specimen or informal privacy screen. Each individual stem blooms once (from the top of the trunk down over several years) and then dies, but the cluster as a whole persists indefinitely as new stems are produced from the base. The fruits and fallen berries contain calcium oxalate crystals that cause intense irritation to skin — collect and dispose of fallen fruit promptly, and wear gloves when working near fruiting clusters. An outstanding specimen palm for sheltered zone 9–12 landscapes.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants or rooted cuttings in spring in any very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; desert honeysuckle requires excellent drainage and lean soil — rich, moist soil causes rank growth and reduced flowering. Cut back hard to 12 inches in late winter to encourage compact, dense growth and maximum summer bloom.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; desert honeysuckle (hummingbird bush) is a native Southwestern shrub with an extraordinarily long season of tubular, orange-red flowers that are among the most powerfully attractive hummingbird plants in the xeric and arid Southwest. Blooming from summer through fall with each successive flowering flush triggered by summer monsoon rains, the plant produces dense masses of 1.5-inch, orange-red tubular flowers that provide rich nectar for migrating and resident hummingbirds. It is extremely drought tolerant and actually declines in fertility and blooming if given rich soil or excess water — thriving in the hard, dry, alkaline conditions of the Southwest and Edwards Plateau. Cut back hard each late winter before new growth begins. The species A. wrightii (flame anisacanthus, Wright's desert honeysuckle) has similar requirements and is equally outstanding as a hummingbird plant; both are outstanding native choices for xeric wildflower and butterfly gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow seed after the last frost or start divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; scarlet rose mallow thrives in consistently wet to boggy conditions and grows naturally in swamps, marshes, and pond edges. In zone 9 it often reblooms from hard pruning in midsummer.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; scarlet rose mallow is one of the most spectacular native perennials of the eastern and Gulf Coast wetlands, producing enormous (6–8 inch) brilliant crimson-red flowers with a deep red eye on tall, robust, deeply palmate-lobed, dramatically architectural plants that grow vigorously in standing water or perpetually wet soil. The flowers have a uniquely delicate, crepe-paper texture and the color is among the most vivid reds in the plant kingdom. Unlike the hybrid hardy hibiscus (H. moscheutos x) which needs drier soil, H. coccineus must have consistently wet or saturated conditions to thrive — it grows naturally in swamps and tidal marshes. It dies back completely to the ground after frost but regrows vigorously from the roots each spring, emerging late (June in zone 9) and quickly reaching its mature height. An outstanding native for rain gardens, pond margins, water features, and wet spots that are otherwise difficult to plant attractively.
